rows.Name: Trigeminal Neuralgia
Etiology: Degeneration or pressure on the trigeminal nerve (from inflammation, infection, trauma, tumor)
Diagnostics: MRI to rule out lesions, vascular abnormalities, MS
EMG can distinguish between symptomatic and classical TN
Clinical Manifestations: Abrupt stabbing pain along nerve branches lasting several seconds to minutes, unpredictable recurring
Branch 3 - Side of tongue, lower lip, jaw
Triggers can include chewing, brushing teeth, cold air, face washing, yawning
Management: Nutrition - high protein, soft foods, chew on opposite side
Hygiene and oral care -lukewarm water, soft cloths and toothbrush, warm mouth rinse
Alternate forms of communication when acute
Treatment: Gabapentin and Pregabalin - treat neuropathic pain
Percutaneous radiofrequency rhizotomy (nerve ablation) - post of care, assessment of: pain, corneal reflex, ocular muscles, hearing, sensation, facial nerve

rows.Name: Bells Palsy
rows.Description: Unilateral paralysis of the facial nerve - CN VII
rows.Etiology: Trauma, compression, or infection (herpes simplex virus - HSV)
Pathophysiology: Usually resolves over weeks but can leave some permanent damage
Onset often acute, affecting muscles of upper and lower face
Diagnostics: Electromyography (EMG) can help predict nerve recovery
Clinical Manifestations: Any or all branches of facial nerve may be affected
Management: Corticosteroids within 72hrs for 2-3weeks – decreases pain and edema
Acyclovir if HSV related for 5 – 10 days
Hot wet compresses can reduce discomfort aid circulation
Artificial tears and sunglasses for affected eye
Therapeutic communication for change in appearance

rows.Name: Seizure Disorders
rows.Description: Group of neurological diseases marked by recurring seizures
rows.Etiology: Often a symptom of an underlying condition
Pathophysiology: Hyperexcitation of neurons leading to abnormal electrical activity
Gliosis often found in area of the brain where the activity arises
Diagnostics: Health history (trauma, tumors, metabolic, alcoholism, toxins, organ failure)
MRI, CT, EEG (difficult to catch in progress)
Clinical Manifestations (General onset): Bilateral synchronous discharges in the brain
Whole brain is affected; no warning / aura
Pt will usually lose consciousness in a few seconds to minutes
This onset is usually a tonic-colonic seizure
Clinical Manifestations (Focal onset): Secondary / generalized seizures may result in temporary neurodeficit in postictal phase
Aware -simple motor or sensory phenomena with no loss of consciousness. Often longer than 1min. Weakness numbness tingling.

Impaired awareness - loss of consciousness or dream like state, can last 1-2min, pt usually confused and tired. May stiffen or jerk, or automatisms like lip smacking. May or may not have an emotional component.
Clinical Manifestations (Tonic Colonic): Body stiffening (tonus) for 10-20s; then extremities jerk (colonus) for 30-40s; repeats
May also see poor respiratory effort
Muscle soreness, fatigue, malaise afterwards (hours to days)
Clinical Manifestations (Status Epilepti: Brain uses more energy than supplied, neuronal exhaustion. Can result in permanent brain damage
Can result in metabolic and cardiopulmonary complications (acidosis, hypoxemia, hyperthermia, dysrhythmias, ventilatory insufficiency)
Benzodiazepines are first line (midazolam, lorazepam, diazepam)
rows.Phase (Prodomal): Signs or activities that provoke a seizure (flashing lights)
rows.Phase (Aural): Sensory warning of impending seizure (headache, visuals)
Management (Acute): Maintain patent airway, support head, turn to side, loosen restrictive clothing
Ease to floor, remove bed rails if not padded
Do NOT put anything in mouth
Establish IV access for benzos or phenytoin
Management (Postictal): Assess neuro status (cognition, GCS) *not pupils initially
Assess for injuries - falls and biting
Treatment: Surgical options but stringent requirements, medication resistant (focal impaired awareness - resection of epileptogenic tissue; generalized - bisect corpus callosum)
Vagal nerve stimulation (electrode placed on vagal nerve, when pt senses seizure imminent they activate, increases blood and NTs to area reducing seizure activity)
Treatment (Preventative): Phenytoin - limits seizures by altering ion transport, can decrease synaptic transmission. (monitor for rash, suicidal thoughts, mental status)
Carbamazepine - decreases synaptic nerve transmission via sodium channels. (monitor for behavior change / SI, CBC, calcium, thyroid; do not take with grapefruit juice, report visual abnormalities)
Phenobarbital - causes cns depression, inhibits neural transmission. (monitor respiratory, pulse, bp, sedation)
Nursing: Reassure and orient patient as needed
Document - time, movement, triggers, postictal response
Teaching: Wear helmet if at risk for head injury
Service dogs can detect upcoming event, alert and support

rows.Name: Multiple Sclerosis
Description: Chronic Progressive Degenerative Autoimmune disorder of the CNS
Characterized by disseminated demyelination of nerve fibers of the brain, spinal cord, and optical nerves
Etiology: Cause unknown, thought to be genetic predisposition with environmental triggers
Might be related to vit D deficiency (more common in northern countries)
rows.Phase (1): Chronic inflammation - unknown trigger activates T-cells to attack neurons in the brain barrier. CNS activates inflammatory response
rows.Phase (2): Demyelination - neurons in brain and spinal cord are attacked by immune system. Myelin sheath is damaged. Nerve axon becomes exposed and disrupted. Loss of oligodendrocytes and proliferation of astrocytes also occurs.
rows.Phase (3): Gliosis - damaged myelin is replaced by glial scar tissue which forms into hard plaques. Nerve conduction is disrupted.
Diagnostics: Need to have 2 demyelinating lesions in 2 different locations in the brain
Lumbar puncture (sample CSF for proteins, oligoclonal IgG, oligoclonal banding means inflammation in spinal cord)
Management: Immunomodulators to reduce exacerbations and slow progression (Interferon 1b and 1A, glatiramer acetate)
Anticholinergics to prevent cramps and spasms
Other medications for symptoms (vertigo, depression, pain)
Stem cell therapy (research is examining this to reduce brain inflammation)
Teaching: Home safety (hand rails, no loose rugs, minimize stairs)

rows.Name: Parkinson’s Disease
rows.Description: A slow, progressive, degenerative disorder of the CNS basal ganglia
rows.Etiology: Unknown cause, genetic component, environmental risks
Risk Factors: A close relative with the disease
Exposure to toxins - herbicides, pesticides, heavy metals carbon monoxide
Some medications (tranquilizers - haloperidol and phenothiazines, antipsychotics)
A single concussion increases risk by 57%
Pathophysiology: Degeneration of dopamine producing neurons in substantia nigra (midbrain)
Dopamine depletion in basal ganglia leads to impaired motor control
Associated with Lewy bodies (abnormal deposits of α-synucleins)
rows.Stages (1): Mild symptoms. Tremors and movement symptoms only on one side. Changes in posture, walking, face.
rows.Stages (2): Symptoms worsen. Tremors, rigidity, movement symptoms on both sides. Still somewhat independent
rows.Stages (3): Mid stage. Loss of balance and slow movement. Falls are more common. Still independent but significant impact, may need help with ADLs
rows.Stages (4): Symptoms are severe and limiting. May be able to stand without assistance but need a walker. Loss of independence
rows.Stages (5): Debilitating stage. Unable to walk or stand, wheelchair or bedridden. May experience hallucinations and delusions.
Clinical Manifestations: Usually begins in middle or older age
Symptoms do not present until 80% of substantia nigra lost
TRAP - Tremor, Rigidity, Akinesia (slowing of autonomic movement), Postural instability
Pill rolling (thumb against index finger tremor)
Management: Medication and Surgical options to slow progression and reduce symptoms
rows.Medication (Levodopa): precursor of dopamine and crosses blood brain, converted to dopamine in basal ganglia
rows.Medication (Carbidopa): inhibits an enzyme that breaks down levodopa before it reaches brain
rows.Medication (Benztropine): anticholinergic, helps rebalance cns in favor of dopamine
Treatment: Ablation therapy - stereotactic thalamotomy; destroys part of the thalamus to block normal brain activity from reaching muscles, reduces tremors
Deep brain stimulation - placing an electrode in thalamus, globus pallidus, or subthalamic nucleus; delivers current to block impulses that cause tremors
Fetal neural tissue transplant into the basal ganglia provides dopamine producing cells
rows.Nursing: Monitor for signs of depression and dementia

rows.Name: Guillain-Barre Syndrome
Description: Form of polyneuritis, impacting peripheral nervous system
Etiology: Unknown, suspected autoimmune response following infection.

Potential triggering pathogens: campylobacter jejuni (intestinal, food poisoning), cytomegalovirus (related to herpes), epstein barr (mono), varicella zoster (related to herpes, chicken pox / shingles)
Pathophysiology: Acute inflammatory demyelinating polyneuropathy is most common type.
As nerves become demyelinated both sympathetic and parasympathetic impacted (means bp, cardio, gi, gu, pupil changes)
rows.Phase (1): Immune system goes into overdrive following an infection, T lymphocytes attack myelin sheath of peripheral nerves
rows.Phase (2): Demyelination progresses, neural impulses are slowed down or stopped. Muscles innervated by PNS will atrophy
rows.Phase (3): Recovery phase. Nerves will remyelinate slowly. Functionality very slowly returns, proximal to distal (head to toe)
Diagnostics: Lumbar puncture (assess protein levels, 7 - 10 days will be elevated)
Clinical Manifestations: Weakness can progress to complete paralysis
Can progress to brainstem impacting cranial nerves - eye movement, dysphagia, facial paresthesia
Assessment: Done frequently as demyelination moves up the body
Management: Oxygen supplement (intubate and ventilate as needed)
Nutrition (aspiration risk, may progress to needing feeding tube or TPN)
Treatment: IVIG (IV immunoglobulins to treat deficiencies in immune response; within 2 weeks of diagnosis)
Plasmapheresis (dialysis to remove antibodies causing autoimmune response; within 3 weeks of onset)
